content.claim: Despite maintaining a nominally comprehensive structure since the 1970s, Finland has experienced rising socio-economic achievement gaps since the 1990s after decentralization and school-choice deregulation, with Helsinki showing increased ethnic and socio-spatial segregation across and within schools through selective admission to language and ability-grouped classes.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Finnish system reform lens
limitations: Finnish erosion of equity is well-documented in Nordic educational-sociology literature but underrepresented in English-language international commentary
Degree of comparability between Finnish decentralization-driven segregation and Hungarian formal early tracking remains unclear

Segregation mechanisms in Finland (residential sorting, selective within-school classes, language-track selection) differ structurally from Hungarian between-school selection, limiting direct parallel
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
